lessons.title: A Crown’s Reach
lessons.summary: Turn every placed crown into useful exclusions.
lessons.explanation: A crown claims its entire row, column, and region. Its eight neighboring cells are also unavailable, including the diagonals.
steps: Find every row, column, and region touched by the crown.
Mark those cells with Xs so only real candidates remain.

example.caption: The crown at B2 rules out its row, column, region, and every touching cell. Gold cells call out diagonal exclusions.

lessons.title: Last Square Standing
lessons.summary: When a unit has one candidate left, crown it.
lessons.explanation: Every row, column, and colored region needs exactly one crown. If exclusions leave just one open square in any of them, that square is forced.
steps: Scan each row, column, and region for open cells.
Crown the only candidate, then apply its new exclusions.

example.caption: Row 3 has only C3 open, so C3 must hold its crown. The gold square is the forced placement.

lessons.title: Locked Intersections
lessons.summary: Use the overlap between a region and a line.
lessons.explanation: If every candidate for a region lies in one row or column, that line’s crown must belong to the region. Other cells on the same line can be excluded.
steps: Find a region whose candidates share one row or column.
Keep those candidates and cross out the rest of that line.

example.caption: Region 1 can only use column A. Its crown claims that column, so A3 and A4 can be crossed out.

lessons.title: Adjacent-Row Support
lessons.summary: Let one row’s candidates reshape the next row.
lessons.explanation: A crown in the neighboring row cannot touch your chosen crown. If a square would touch every candidate in that row, the square is impossible.
steps: Locate a row with candidates clustered in nearby columns.
In the adjacent row, exclude cells touching every candidate.

example.caption: Row 1 must crown B1 or C1. B2 and C2 touch both possibilities, so neither can hold row 2’s crown.

lessons.title: Royal Pairs
lessons.summary: Reserve two columns for two constrained rows.
lessons.explanation: When two rows can place their crowns only in the same two columns, those columns are reserved. Every other row can exclude both columns.
steps: Find two rows whose candidates occupy the same two columns.
Cross those columns out in every other unfinished row.

example.caption: Rows 1 and 2 must use columns B and C between them. Those columns are unavailable to rows 3 and 4.

lessons.title: Proof by Contradiction
lessons.summary: Test a candidate and follow its consequences.
lessons.explanation: When direct deductions stall, suppose a candidate were crowned. If that assumption leaves a row, column, or region with no possible crown, the candidate must be excluded.
steps: Choose one candidate and temporarily treat it as a crown.
Apply only certain deductions. If a unit becomes impossible, reject the assumption.

example.caption: Assuming A1 is a crown would also exclude B2. Row 2 would have nowhere left, so A1 must be crossed out.
